Okay, so let's talk sort of around copywriting. Oh, thank you very much. Give the old man his first, but I am gonna want one. So, sort of the first thing you and I agreed that we would talk about is some underlying fundamental insight into the psychology of the buyer. The person we are actually trying to get to read our copy, respond to our copy, buy things from us, et cetera. So, what's your overarching position on who this person is and sort of where they start? Okay, first, is anybody mad at me from last night? I think so. What do you care? Just do it. Yeah, just. So, as anybody got, as anybody spent time studying psychology, got a degree in it or just dabbled in a pop psychology, anybody hung out with old school street-wide salesman? That would be the main thing. Really, so, okay. Well, I accidentally got a. Well, let me give you a better one. No, I'll help you. So, how many people in here have actually sold hardcore, nose to nose, toes to toes, on the living room floor, in a showroom, et cetera? Okay, then. Now, keep your hands up if you actually cashed a commission check. I mean, you actually like made some money doing it. Okay, see, they're not hopeless. Okay, good. There you go. Yeah, most, I accidentally got a degree in psychology, a four-year degree from the University of California, and I graduated into the teeth of the 74 recession, and absolutely nothing I learned there was of any value. There were 20,000 BAs in psychology given out of the United States that year for one job that was available. I think I've learned that this is a childcare worker in Sacramento for a couple years. Anyway, did you know, by the way, that was on the test you take in high school, I was supposed to be a social worker? Oh, really? Yeah. That was, I'm serious. In a way, no. I'm serious, that's what the, you know that aptitude test thing you take? I was supposed to be a social, hey, I'd have been efficient. I could handle a big case load. Get a job, next. I mean, you know. Stop whining, yeah. Yeah, so I took a interest in psychology into my life as a freelance copywriter and hung out with the street-wise salesman and really paid attention to old school psychology. So when I started writing direct response to advertising, it was a very short move for me to go from looking at the audiences out there in a target market and then looking kind of beneath the surface and looking at what I believed they needed to happen to be able to move them off of their inaction. And when I described it later in, when I started writing about this, I describe people in the average market as a synambulistic blob welded to the couch so firmly it wouldn't leave to save its ass if the house was burning down. And your job as an ad-rivers, you get them to take out their wallet and actually take some action. So this is what I was talking about last night with how easy it can be to get somebody up on the fence, to get somebody to agree with you that yeah, you got a good product and maybe someday down the road I'll buy it. But you got to get them to act and that's where the pro is separated from the rookie. You know, that's why, I'm reasonably certain you agree, that's why I push people to study the old direct response so much. I say there's old and the gold because they had a much tougher job at what he just described really than we do now. Because they actually did have to get them up off the couch. I mean, somebody had to go get a checkbook out of the kitchen drawer, write out a check, fill out a coupon, put it in an envelope, not talk themselves out of it, get a postage stamp and drop it in a mailbox in order to respond. And then finally they were able to respond by phone when 800 numbers and the ability to take a credit card over the phone. But that's actually, that's more recent than it is ancient. Sugarman was the first guy to put an 800 number in a direct response ad and be able to take orders by credit card over the phone. So that's what, 30, 30, 30. Early 70s, yes. Yeah, early 70s. So I know for some of you that's ancient history, but not for John and I and not for many. I mean, it's not like it was at the turn of the century for God's sakes. So those people doing direct response then had a much higher hurdle to move this person, John just described, and I generally share the same disdainful idea of where they are. Well, there's a subtle balance there. It's not, don't get the idea that I don't like my customer. I like him a lot. In fact, one of the things that I try to teach people to come to me for advice just on life and stuff is that salesmen lead better lives. People who know how to sell and understand what Dan and I are talking about, the ability to get somebody to move and actually buy something, you lead a better life because you can't do that if you don't live firmly in reality. And the vast majority of people live kind of on the surface of life, kind of bobbing around like Plotsman Jetson on the surface of the ocean, taking hither and two, by the waves. And entrepreneurs and who are good salesmen actually start to control your life. You have goals, you go forward. You are the engine of your success. You move yourself forward. That is not shared by the vast majority of people out there. They are living in very shallow lives. Salesmen lead better lives all the way around. You don't look at the world the way you wish it was or the way you think it ought to be. You look at the way the world the way it is. And people get shocked when they first start thinking about this because they take a look. Does that mean I have to despise my customer base? No, you actually start to love people more when all of the nonsense falls away. All of the BS is taken away. And you actually deal with people for who they are and how they will actually act as opposed to the way you think they should or ought to act. And it changes the whole dynamic. And where they start. So I think that one of the mistakes, and so salespeople, you learn not to start at a different place than the prospect is because you get feedback, right? You sit across the kitchen table. You sit across the desk in the office. And if they're over here and you start here, you pretty quickly get a feel of that and you sort of back up and retrench. We don't get that feedback when we sell through media. And a very common mistake is making assumptions about where they are and starting further along than they are in the process towards making a decision about whatever it is that you are putting in front of them. So like what you just said, that we have our own engines and our engines start pretty quick. They either don't have their own engine or it's still got a hand crank. And so we got to start way back with them there, not like ready to hear the good news about the thing we have to offer them. And Dan, do you remember the first time you met somebody who was a customer who you had not met before and they were a little astonished that you didn't know them because they felt they knew you so well. Yes. They actually felt they had. Which is what's supposed to happen, right? But that moment that happens is you realize now you've arrived as a marketer because the communication that you just affected with them might've been a year ago and they bought and then you've got more things going back and forth. They're reading your newsletters, they're reading yourself. They feel that connection on a visceral level. And how many people feel you know Dan after reading them these years before you met them? So that's a major step in moving from being a social entrepreneur to having the unlimited potential. And I'd like to add one other side to that psychology thing that I had. For myself, I operated from a gun to the head position. And I found out later that Halbert did the same thing and J.A. Abraham did the same thing. I don't know if you did. But I had no backup. I had no freelance. I'd never met a freelancer when I became one. I was making up the gig as I was going along. And I was one of the few freelancers down in Los Angeles in the 80s because it just wasn't an occupation that was well thought of or even that a lot of people knew about. So I had to make up things as I was going. One of the things I did was I imagined a gun was to my head when I was writing an ad that would go off if the ad didn't work. And how would I react to that? So would I choose that headline? Would I take that tact in my sales pitch? Would I tell a joke or would I be straightforward? And over and over again, that idea of my life is on the line if this ad doesn't work kept me really focused on classic salesmanship. So I didn't experiment because when it came down to it, writing for a client, I already had the money or I was going to get paid whether he had worked or not. So in a lot of writers, you can get lazy. Rather than that, I kept thinking, what's the one thing I would do if my life really, really depended on that? So the gun to the head philosophy of working erases a lot of the BS that can leak into your writing when you think, well, why not try this? Well, because it won't work. One of the evils of the internet is that it has caused marketers to dramatically increase the quantity of their output to their customers and made them therefore quick, sloppy, and casual about a lot of it. Whereas really, most people would be better off putting 75% less out and really working on the 25% to get it right. I mean, John's absolutely right. I mean, those of us, and of course, once is a freelancer, once you're in the game for royalties, not just fees, then from a monetary standpoint, you actually care whether or not this stuff works, not just from an ethical standpoint or a professional reputation standpoint. But I'm often more concerned that it works than the client is even. And yeah, you work on it like this is life or death, and this baby's gotta produce or else. And I think a lot of marketers do not do that. They are like, okay, we're gonna get two emails out a day this week and we're gonna do it every day for five days, let's slap these babies together and let's push the button. And I think it's a mistake. And one of the things that I know you've come up with, come up against with clients, which I do, is that you would write the ad that you believe needs to be run to do it. And everything that you know says this ad needs to be run. What's the first thing the client says? My daughter, the English lit major, read it and thinks you ought to change the headline now, use this word and do this. And if you don't have control as a freelancer, one of the first things I teach freelancers is to become the adult in the room. You control the situation. You are not a vendor, you are in control. You do that by charging massive amounts of money. And firing people. And one of the things I used to do in the agency, so I walk in and I said, I won't work with you guys until you fire the designer. Because the designer had way too much seen what the ad looked like. He wanted gray floating blocks of copy under his designs. And just back in the 80s, before direct response had reestablished itself. That's working. See, John, the chief point here is that there are reliable things to do. And they are based on behavioral. So I say direct response is roughly half behavioral psych and half math. That's really what it is. And so there's reliable psychological principles. There's reliable sales principles. And yes, somebody periodically innovates. But it's really not necessary to be the innovator in order to make a lot of money in this field. There is a fairly predictable, reliable path of elements that make things work. And they really haven't changed. Media's changed. Technology's changed. But a presentation that works is a presentation that works. It's a presentation that works. And you and I, as writers, what were we just talking about in the hallway before we came in? If we want to be creative, we'll go right on a freaking knot on the outside. We'll go right on out. Although it turns out, truth be told, so that's not that creative a process either. So I mean, when I got an advertising, I thought it was a creative exercise. And it is not that at all. It is a methodical exercise. It turns out fiction is harder. Yes, harder and also methodical. There are formulas. There are architectures. There are reliable things. I want to shift gears because I'm going to watch the clock. So I'd like you to talk a little bit about the whole circle to desk thing. What goes on really before we get a word on paper? I did two things when I started writing for myself. And really, being a freelancer is an entrepreneurial gig. I was, you know, it was my own business. And I had to figure out how to make sure I was doing it and doing it right. So I made a couple of decisions. One was business before pleasure, which for a slacker changed my life. That's all I did is I wasn't going to. I could still have a drink with the boys and party and do whatever. But I had to finish all the business first. So that was huge. But then what I did is I started thinking, wait a minute. I need to really nail down this working environment for me. And I needed every trick in the book on my side. So I actually wore writing clothes. How many people have writing clothes or a hat or something that they do? OK, you know, you've heard people talk about put on your writer hat or something or put on your salesman hat. I literally did that. And it helped. Now, one of the reasons that helped is because my writing clothes are dirty sweats and an old t-shirt. So I actually couldn't go to the door and answer it or walk outside to the thing. I was stuck in there unless I was going to change clothes. And I actually had different hats on that I would do. I'd put a different hat on when I would call a client. I'd put a different hat on when I was ready to sit down and actually face the, I started out with a typewriter. That's how old I freaking am. But, you know, when I was standing in a monitor or a blank sheet of paper. And what I developed, what Dan was mentioning there, is this habit of getting into the mood to write. How many people, I told you that writer's block doesn't exist last night, but how many people have trouble gearing up to sit down and actually write the stuff you need to write? Yeah, it's because your subconscious isn't ready. Right. And it's exactly. And that's why what I started doing is, I like dogs. I notice that dogs circle things. They circle around before they lay down. And I started circling the desk. And I'd actually worked myself into a froth. Now, I didn't approach the desk until I was ready to write. And that's why I never encountered writer's block. So what Dan was talking about last night, I had all my ducks in a row. I knew what I was going to do. So when I sat down, I wasn't thinking, well, where do I start? I knew where to start. I knew who I was going after. I knew what the pain points were. I knew what the feature benefits were. And by the way, when I write, I usually write the bullets first and then move to a headline and then start writing. So I construct the ad in a kind of like building a house, or remodeling a house. But I would circle the desk and work myself into a froth. And one of the things that I thought when I was sitting down, I would think, god damn it, there are people walking around out there today that have money in their wallet that belongs to me. And the universe will not be right until I rectify that situation. Thank you very much. Dogs also pee to mark their territory. I hope you were not doing that in your office. Dan, you promised you wouldn't bring that part of the time. I wear astronaut underwear when I write. That work area probably got smelly after a length of time. So I hear from people all the time, your hands went up a lot, that for you writing is hard. And oh, how can I do a newsletter once a month or saleslet? If writing and writing at a decent speed is difficult for you, it is because your preparation is poor. That's just that simple. And one or both kinds of preparation. So one is the pragmatic preparation of having a profile of who you're writing to, having some sort of template in place, having some good swipe files until you get so old and accomplished at it like he and I are where I imagine you like me now. 90% of the time you're working out of the swipe files in your head, not the swipe files in the back room, although they are still there. But so there's that kind of preparation. Am I practically ready to write? And then there is your subconscious in gear. Does it know that that's what's supposed to be happening right now? And almost every copywriter has some sort of ritualistic behavior, not necessarily the one he described, but some sort of ritualistic behavior. They have an environment. I mean, real copywriters aren't writing at Starbucks. The world thinks that's their office somehow. And Schultz has convinced everybody that that's their office or Panera bread, for God's sakes. Real copywriters have an environment that they have organized, a place. And not only is it because their stuff is there and they're maybe protected from disruptions, it's because their subconscious knows when they enter that place, oh, OK, I got to flip open the drawers. I got to turn on the switches. This is now all about writing. And so when it's difficult for you, it's really a matter of preparation more than it is you can't write. Writing is hard. You don't have a good idea. And let me help some of you out who do have this trouble. Just go ahead and develop your own routines. You can do whatever you want. There are two kinds of writers, those who work in complete silence, I work in complete silence, and those who play music in the background. Stephen King wrote with ACVC, Blair, and all that. I have a TV on. OK, so. I have a television on. There's a number of writers. Over here, I don't see it. Exactly. So it's like white noise, so it does that. So don't worry about that. Choose that either one or the other. But get your routine down so that you do that. Take the phone off the hook and make a big deal about taking the phone off the hook. Nobody has hooks. We're both phone off hook. Say, phone off hook, typewriter. Thanks for the ad. Yeah. Actually, I had a wooden chair. I'm sensitive to this, because I have this titanium member who has no frame of reference for any of this stuff. So like 20 times during a mastermind meeting, I have to explain to her what the object is I'm talking about. The color adjustment knob on the other side. Exactly. Yeah, exactly. But get your. So get your. God damn, I hadn't thought about that in a long time. A color adjustment. Wow. Shut up. Whoa. I found a picture of myself in the 50s. And the first TV you have was round. That was a move up from the one that reflected off the mirror when you opened. Anyway, what was I talking about? Oh, and for your routine, whatever you need to do, but get the routine down. It can be a two minute routine or a five minute routine, whatever you do, but set it down. But make it so that nobody's going to disturb you and then set a clock. This was a big break. This is big. This is very big. Yeah. Start with 50 minutes out of an hour. Work for two hours with 10 minute breaks. So work for 50 minutes, and then take a 10 minute break, and literally leave. Get out of that. And when you come back, do your routine again to get back into it. We're stylistically different on that. So like I take no breaks. But find out. Right. But I write to a deadline just like you do. I mean, if you don't have a stop point, if you don't have a stop point that you're working toward, your subconscious just doesn't know when it needs to be done. It's astonishing what an alarm clock. And I would get an old school alarm clock, you know, or you can use your iPhone and it goes off and it does that little drum beat or whatever. See, now you're making adjustments. See, I made you hypersensitive about that. So yeah. Actually, I have a dog that wakes up and has to go pee every hour. There you go. Perfect. I'm sleeping on my feet. But find out, and it could be your 45 minutes and 15 minutes. Could be your half an hour and half an hour. Mine's more like two hours and then half an hour, and then back for two hours, and then I'm done. Four hours of writing is more, if you're steady productive, in four hours of writing, you get more done than the average market gets done in a month. But you want to dictate end times, and I dictate the amount of work that's going to be done by that end time. So if it's 16 pages and it's been assigned 50 minutes, my entire system now knows that it's got 50 minutes to do 16 pages. And one of the big things I remember, that you cemented down, I was kind of already in that camp. But I tend to be a perfectionist sometimes. I know it's hard to believe, but sometimes I do. And Dan used to preach, good enough is good enough. I don't know if you already talked about it yesterday or not. But that is really important. Know when to stop. And in short, it's not perfect. Perfect to add seldom work anyway. And often what you're doing is you're messing with what's going to be a 1% difference. That's right. It's incremental. So when it's good enough, that's when you pump. That's why Dan does two edits, and he's done. I will do more, but I'm working sometimes for clients who demand that perfection. They don't want the occasional typo in there. But most ads, we used to purposely put typos in our printed ads and our direct mail. So if it was perfect, it still didn't look perfect. But you can get that just by doing two less edits usually. OK, that's a tangent. So last night, I know you lived on USP. However, maybe to revisit or extend or give you another bite at that apple, I think one-legged golf course is an all-time classic. I think you're one of the best at getting to the big idea, what Nicholas called the hidden benefit, the hook, the thing that really makes a pitch stand apart from all other pitches in its category. OK, how many people have seen my one-legged golf ad? That was a big breakthrough for me because of two things. One was I had a client who I wasn't going to work for. I was actually having my second or third midlife crisis and was playing in biker bars in this band that I formed. I didn't want to work at all. So they lured me to come back to work. They promised they would take the leash off. And what that meant was that they were going to run anything I wrote. They would mail it, and they would put it into magazines, and they'd swallow hard, and they would not change a word. And that to a writer who has worked with clients who has always changed his stuff was like catnip. I mean, it was just, OK, so we didn't did it. So I just threw myself into this knowing that there was no limit to what I could do. I mean, I could ruin these guys if I wanted to, but my goal was to make them very successful, which I was able to do. And so what I did is what Dan was talking about yesterday, again, the prep part. I started interviewing clients. And one of the first products they had was a golf guy who had this strange swing that, if you adopted the swing, and they were selling videotapes. That's going back, not even DVDs. So on videotape, they just filmed this guy doing this swing, and I was supposed to sell this videotape, an instructional golf video. It was kind of new back then. This is probably 1991, I think, something. And so I thought, that's pretty boring. There's not a lot going on there. There's nothing for me to get my teeth around. So I interviewed what we call the talent, the guy behind the thing. I said, I was talking to him for about an hour on the phone. Wacky guy, but no stories came out. And finally, I said, how did you come up with this triple coil swing that you call? He says, ah, that old story. Nobody wants to hear that. I was golfing one day behind a four-sum. One of the guys only had one leg. And as he hopped up to the first tee to swing, I thought, for sure, he's going to fall over. But he didn't. He had this perfect swing, and he knocked a 300-yard drive. And I had this epiphany at the point. And I just said, stop. And I said, I pretty much got it. So this is what inspired him. He says, yeah, but nobody wants to hear that stuff. Here's what's really important about this, because we have this experience with clients all the time. They've got something spectacular. And it's old hat to them. They don't know it has any value, which means you have something like that, too. I have yet to find anybody that doesn't, that with enough probing, they're sitting on a story they ought to be telling. Tomorrow morning, I'm going to talk about story, by the way. They're sitting on a story they ought to be telling that they either don't like, or they are embarrassed by, yet they should be using it. They feel it's treating on something. They have cancer recovery. They feel it's treating on something like that. They don't put any value on it, whatever. They've got it, which means you've got it. Dan, this goes back to when I used to deal with clients. If I gave them an ad, and they said, this is great. Can't wait to run it. I knew, bomb. This thing was not going to work. I want a client so nervous that the only reason he runs the ad is because he's paying me so much money. It would be the sunk cost theory is eating away at him, because they don't want to do it. And one of the ways you get to that is what Dan was just alluding to. I would interview the secretaries, the feed, the street salesman, everybody else, but the guy I was dealing with. Because I would get an edited, dream-like story from the guy who owned the company. I want to hear the gossip, the rumors. I want to hear what the guys who have to actually handle the refunds coming in. I want to talk to the people who are doing customer support. And often, the best stuff you'll get is just right outside his office. They're going, oh, yeah, he told you that. That's bullshit. So you'll like the story. We'll bubble up. So you'll like this. And I don't think I've told you. So a few years ago, I did a bunch of work for Miracle, the hearing aid company. They spent $2 million with me, by the way, and used almost nothing that I gave them. I'm sure, sign you did it right. Oh, yeah, it was perfect. The franchisees that did use it kicked butt, but corporate set themselves on fire. But that's neither here nor there. So Miracle, their sales hearing aids, they got about 1,200 stores across the country. Interesting thing about the company is the original founder, the guy that started it, has sold it, bought it back for less than he sold it for, sold it, bought it back to big dumb companies four times in his lifetime. The only reason he's not doing it a fifth time is he's 92 years old. So he sells it to a big dumb company, they screw it all up, he buys it back, he fixes it, he turns it back into a direct sales company, and builds it back up, sells it to some big dumb company. So when I was working for them, they had just come out of being owned by Bosch and Loam, who thought hearing aids were eyeglasses, which they aren't. And it's more like erectile dysfunction pills than it is eyeglasses for the male buyer. I mean, it's a big emotional deal. It's, you know, we're old, yeah. So here's the deal. So I'm interviewing franchise owners on the phone because I don't trust anything the corporate client is telling me, right? Just like I wouldn't trust anything you're telling me, in part because you got a belief system that builds up about your business and your customers and so forth, that you become emotionally committed to, that often is not reality, in part because you got some asset like this that you don't know you've got, that you don't value. So as John said, you got to go poke around other places. And if you're doing it for yourself, you got to be willing to like kind of wipe the slate clean as best you can of all your biases and start doing the same poking around as if you had brought yourself in as an outsider, hard but not impossible. So I'm interviewing these franchise owners. And I don't know, I'm on the phone for like an hour with like eight, 10 of them. And the one I'm on the phone with used to be president of the company. And now he's at one time and now he's retired and he's got one little unit in Bumlunk, Alabama and he plays a lot of golf, you know, so. But so he starts telling me about the founder which nobody has told me about the founder. But he was president when the founder was running, one of the times the founder was running the company. He goes, yeah, so he starts telling me about the founder. So now understand a hearing aid, especially to a male, you're selling to a 60 to 80 year old guy, it's a big emotional deal. These are people who don't go to doctors unless they're drug there. They still are big self-reliance guys. This is a big. And damn it, the people would just speak up. This is a big admission, yeah. But it's also, it's a big admission of weakness and frailty and age that nobody wants to make, right? So the founder of this company, his name is Ken Dahlberg. So Ken is a, this is a real life Audie Murphy or a real life John Wayne if you want a more contemporary reference. So he's a World War II fighter pilot, this guy. Shot down twice, once escaped from France on a bicycle under barbed wire, smuggled out by people who rescued him from the field. He's got a purple heart, he's shot down, he's got some award for shooting down more planes than anybody else on the planet. I mean, he crawls out from under the barbed wire, gets home and the first thing he does is sign up for the next mission. I mean, this guy's a real life World War II hero, right? He's still getting a royalty on the product because every time he sells the company, he keeps a royalty. So he's still getting money. So he'll do stuff. They, first of all, they think he's an idiot, which he isn't. Second, they think nobody wants to hear that story. I'm here at this, I'm going, that's the voice. That's the story, right? Cause now we can make this about doing something courageous, cause he wears them, right? That this is really a courageous thing to do, not a weak thing to do, we can put it in his voice. They're not using him at all. They don't even have to go rent a celebrity. They got one and they're not even using the guy. And if I hadn't made the phone calls and I hadn't talked to the franchise owners, I probably never would have found it. Cause you had to go back and company literature almost 50 years to find it being used. Cause gradually everybody around them convinced them to stop telling the story and he didn't want to tell the story anymore and so forth. And we find this again and again and again. And I promise you, you've got assets like this that you aren't leveraging. It's a good exercise amongst each other, by the way, in mastermind groups. It's a good thing, it's a good place to do it, is to probe each other. I don't mean physically necessarily, but. One small caveat to that. The one leg of golfer ad that I wrote has been ripped off I think more than almost any other ad and it's still running by the way. I still collect royalties on that every month. That ad is now going on over 20 years old. It worked fine when the web came along and it's been in mail and it still appears in tight markets that we're gonna. But people rip it off and you can't like use that for an accountant. You can't say the amazing secrets of a one-legged accountant that will help you beat the IRS at their own game. There has to be relevance. So what Dan was talking about with this guy, he found the hook, the hook between this guy's experience to the problem that people were having with admitting that they needed to wear the hearing aids. If you get that guy, they never ran it. I can't believe it. A lot of the work I did for them, some. It's getting moldy in a file. Some of the smaller franchise owners used it. Corporate pretty much went in and backed out. But anyway, so clients are a topic. Clients, all clients suck. All clients suck. I had that written above my desk for 20 years. Some are worse than others. And when I started working for myself, I sucked. I was the worst client I ever had in my life. So that's, for a entrepreneur, just realize that. Suddenly all the stuff that I forced my clients to do for 30 years, I wasn't willing to do when I was doing my own stuff. I can't use that head. I would have bought my reputation. Well, that's the first, so I call it the entrepreneurial transition. That's when you realize you have a really bad manager. You have a really bad employee. You are both of them and nobody can get fired. This is awful. You're related to both of them. Yeah, I mean, it's worth always having at least one other employee, so you have somebody periodically you can fire. Or at least fantasize about fire. So, biggest in your entire career, biggest single discovery, thing that surprised you, break through... Well, I'd have to say that was, for me, when I started out and I told you guys last night how I went to the Torrance Municipal Library and read everything in the Dewey Decimacy, 600, 820, whatever it was. And the things that I pulled out of there that I liked were old books and a lot of them were out of print. And that turned, and one of the books that really, really hit me hard was Claude Hopkins' Scientific Advertising and My Life in Advertising. Old Stowe's published as a single book, but it's two different books he wrote. And 1921, David. 1921. Yeah. So, and even then when I found it, I thought, well, to me, this was an insight into the kind of salesmanship that I wasn't finding because the other books I was pulling off of the shelf were much more recent, more like textbooks, and they talked about stuff that didn't sound good, but it was theory, it wasn't how to do it. And here's this guy from, you know, half a century ago that was telling me all this. And what happened was me liking that became my entry into meeting when I met Jay Abraham and Gary Halbert and Joe Surgerman. It was their favorite books too. And we had this little cabal of people who the things that we were using were books that were out of print. When I was working with Gary, we paid $1,000 for a third generation mimeograph of the Robert Collier Letter Book. I don't know if that's one of the ones you recommended. Yeah, and now you can get it on Amazon, by the way. Well, you know, what happened was when we started doing the seminars, guys like Aletti and stuff, you know, they got these for nothing, and are now publishing them, so they're all back in print. But this stuff was out of print. I mean, Howard made that trek to the National Library of Congress and spent years in their reading, not years, spent weeks in their reading out of print books that you couldn't otherwise find. He just found these things by Kennedy. Who was that guy, Kennedy, and the... John E. Yeah, the original guys. And what was, I mean, just the discovery that that was my connection to these guys, and of course, the next intellectual thing is that that really means there's a connection between us and cavemen who are selling stuff to each other. I forget who it was that found in an obscure text that they were used chariot salesmen in Rome. Well, so seriously, there was a national, people think USA Today was the first national newspaper. There was a national newspaper during the Civil War called Harper's Weekly, not to be confused with Harper's Magazine, which came later. And it was a national newspaper, like USA Today is today. And so I have replicate issues, you can buy replicate issues now, of this newspaper, and the classified section is full of lead generation ads for Get Rich in Real Estate, how to get a job in sales, right? And stuff that you could buy and resell, be a direct salesperson and make money, and then direct response ads for baldness cures, and a lot of the products, by the way, so like there's an ad in a Civil War era Harper's newspaper for the electric vibrating hairbrush, which stimulates your follicles and somehow regrows hair. There is a current ad in the Sharper Image Catalog for the same stupid product that doesn't do anything. And by the way, if you lay it side by side with the Harper's ad from the Civil War, there's enormous similarity. However, the Civil War ad is better, right? So these fundamentals, and that's the thing that should be really encouraging to everybody, especially anybody that's sort of new to info marketing, to direct marketing, is they are locked in place fundamentals that you can absolutely rely on, that really nothing has changed. And people all the time, so I'm gonna jump to that. So people always think the next new media changes the rules, right? And so we've lived long enough, we've been through this with media that has come and gone, but most- Remember 900 numbers? 900 numbers, see most people, that was the big, that was like everybody talking about Facebook now, everybody was talking about 900 numbers. There were seminars, there were training programs, there were speakers running around teaching- And we told them over and over again, it's gonna be sex lines, guys. Yep. But they all wanted to have latest sports scores, weather. Which pretty much, by the way, still is what e-commerce is too. It's, I mean, it's 80% sex and 20% the rest of us. But so here's my question, specific to all the online media, okay? How do you feel your skills, your methodology, your approach is antiquated by any of that, or has to be altered by any of that? What has it changed any of the rules you live by? Yes, the physical rules. I cut my teeth as a A-list copywriter by writing for the largest mailers in the United States, like Rodale and things like that. So I got to the point where I would start writing, and when I was done, I would either have an eight page or a 12 page or a 16 page direct mail letter. And it was because I'd just trained myself so long. And the reason you go eight or 12 or 16 is because that's how they were printed. It was called the four page signatures would actually go through the physical print. Couldn't be a 15 pager, because that would be a blank page on the back. And if I went one page over a, I think it was four pages plus a brochure or some other things, it would pretty much be an ounce. And then you'd start getting, you'd pay for each little thing that would go above that. So the physical nature of that was very real. The same with newspaper ads, of course, you had a certain amount of space. You could only drop the point size so far before it became unreadable, and you kept packing stuff in there. The web removed that to a large degree, much to the chagrin of the futures for a lot of marketers who realized I mean, Dave and I have talked about this. In 2004 or something, a number of the marketers that were actually selling a lot online, they were newbies in markets. They was low hanging fruit. They were writing 100 page sales letters because they just didn't know when to stop. There was no deadline, there was no reason to stop. They could write whatever they want. One URL could be as many pages as you could fit. So it erased that. But I find that when I write for online things in a print ad, it still comes down to what is essentially an eight or 12 page letter because I can cover everything I need to know. So the discipline is gone. That would be the main thing. And video sales letters actually have reintroduced discipline because you can't go on forever in a video. Although- And of America, 92 minutes. You can go a long way, but a lot of the ones that are working- The last I heard was 29 minutes on average for direct response video, sales videos. Not Lee Jen, but sales videos. I think it was John Benson was talking about the 29 minute. So I may be wrong by a few minutes. That's a long time. That's a long time. But it is still a concise time. And they got there from testing. They tested it out. So there are differences. Kind of like when, you know, the one thing that I learned from Dan was when infomercials came around. That was a new medium they came. It was on TV. Now it wasn't new to advertise on TV. And infomercials actually went back to the birth of the medium. The early shows in the 50s were all infomercials. So Johnny Carson was an infomercial. You know, the dancing cigarette boxes. I better not talk about this stuff. You guys have no idea what I'm talking about. But infomercials weren't new. But the form that came about because of cable TV did change things because there were concise periods of time to get in. And you were like one of the inventors of the thing. Close. But you were there with the guys who invented the blue screen. And then they decided a blue screen has to appear every 15 minutes. And then it was every 10 minutes. And they started testing. And pretty soon it became very concise. The first infomercials I wrote, we had a client, Gary and I, they bought scrap TV time at a channel in Los Angeles. We went in and wrote the thing as we went, had us set there. And the guy would bring on. You know who this guy is. Cal Marves. Yeah. This guy would bring on a guy to sell something. And he'd just talk to them. And when they were done, because they knew they had a 45 minute or an hour or a 29 minute slot to fill, for free that night on BET. So they would stop. And the first one I saw, I started talking to the guy. And I was, wait, we're recording? And he goes, yeah, yeah, come on, go. They left the editor. No editing went straight from when they were. And then when it was over, he said, OK, that's done. And they slammed it up on BET that night for free, because BET didn't realize that time was so valuable. It was 2 AM. And they thought, nobody's going to pay or watch at 2 AM. You could have it. And so that was a wild period of time. But then from that, no rules. It started, people tested. And the cable networks became real, realized that that time was very valuable. So you see these things go through this. The web has gone through that same process. Now it's being much more circumscribed in a way, from testing, by the way, which is the essence of director smart. Well, that's the best thing about it, because it's a great place to test. For cheaper. But I still run it. It's the only indicative. It's not predictive, right, is what I always say. Because if you move something from one media to the next, it may have worked or not worked in one, but it could have life in another. I get so many clients that are online that couldn't be and they brag about how big their list is. It's like, I got 70,000 people on my list. How many of those are buyers? I don't know. Seven. I became a cashmillioner with a list of 300 people first. Get the numbers right. Get the math right, guys, that what's going on. What did you say it was? Half behavioral psych, half math. Most people don't like the math, by the way. They don't like that part of the process. But the math is everything. But you need to know what numbers count and what don't. I still get questions like, what percentage response should I expect to get from my whatever? I don't know. If you sell something for a million dollars, you only need one response out of whatever size your list is. It's one of the all time dumbest questions. It's the most frequently asked question. It doesn't matter, right? Only ROI matters. But most clients, including good, smart clients, I can usually stump them with five questions. I mean, it's just amazing how few people know the numbers of their business. What does it cost you to get a customer from Source A versus Source B? What was the customer worth over 12 months from Source A versus Source B? People don't know this stuff. And they're making decisions in the dark. When you had that slide of the test, headline A to offer B, how many different permutations did that come up? Oh, well, I just stopped. But I mean, you could very easily have a matrix with 200, 250 different. But what's interesting is that you get somebody who's all math, and they will take a bad headline with bad offer and do all this testing and keep coming up with zero. So sell one thing first, move through. And that's where the guys that know the behavioral psych understand they can have a really good offer and a really good headline. But what happens with testing is that you get an old school guy who doesn't want to do the numbers. And somebody else comes in and actually says, did you try testing against that headline or that offer? And he says, no, I'd go by my gut. Those tests usually prove that guy wrong at some point. And all ads tire out. So you can never rely on a single ad. So you've got to get hit to the stuff. Behavioral psychology and math. Stuff works out faster because of online and online. Not always, but a lot of times. I mean, that's a major problem I ran into as a freelancer too. When you're running an ad, you will get tired of it before the math tires out. Absolutely. So you've got to be aware. Let the math tell you when it's time to change the ad or do something. Don't try to circumvent the natural life cycle of it. We had, you remember my Go By The Inch show? We had Go By The Inch on for 8 and 1 half years. And after six months, almost every week, I had a conversation with Len where he's called up, we got to do a new show. So why do we got to do a new show? Well, the numbers are the same. Then why do we have to do? Everybody's seen it. Said, no. If you would quit getting up at 3 o'clock in the morning to watch your own damn show, you would not think everybody's seen it. And we ran that show for 8 and 1 half years without a single change in it and still making money to the bitter end. We have seven minutes. Last question. You kind of went there with some authors, but people often ask, and so it's a good question, who, two, three, four major influencers of how you approach selling and copy that people might want to Amazon, Google, Go Hunt for and a used bookstore, that sort of thing. Yeah, I was really lucky. And I happened to live in Los Angeles at a time when the New York agencies opened up direct response branches in Los Angeles. It was a golden age back in the year. They're all out of business now. But I was the guy that would sneak in the back door because their staff didn't understand direct response. And they didn't want the clients to see me because I am who I am. So they sneak me and I do that writing. But living down there at that time was Jay Abraham. And I met him because he pissed me off and I went to his office to hit him. He had run an ad in the Los Angeles Times looking for direct response copywriters. And I sent one. He sent me back a form letter saying, you really need to read Claude Hopkins if you're going to get into this biz. And I had already devoured everything of that. So I went. I was really ticked off that he hadn't even looked at my litter or my stuff. We wound up being great friends. And I traded hanging around his office for doing work for him. So I worked for free. But my payment was being able to hang out there. So I got to watch him do consultations. And that was the first time I saw it. This is a long time ago when $2,000 meant something. People pay $2,000 an hour to fly into Los Angeles and go and sit in his office while he would sometimes exercise and just pontificate. And it was worth it at the time because there was no other source for that stuff. At Jay's divorce party, I met Gary Halbert. Gary Halbert, when I met him, was the most arrogant, dismissive, bastard I'd ever met in my life. And I liked him immediately. And we became fast friends. I started out. He was a mentor. And then we worked together. And we were lifelong friends until he died a couple of years ago. And through Jay, I met John Finn. I don't know how much you knew John Finn. I knew John a little. He was the only agent for direct response freelance copywriters in the world. He just invented it. He said, somebody needs to be an agent. So I met him. And he started hooking me up with people. And I started ghost writing for a couple of writers who became very, very famous. One is Jim Rutz, who is a very interesting guy. But the thing about Jim is that he taught me discipline. He made me, I wrote a perfect piece. I ghost wrote for him, essentially. I wrote the, he got the deal. And he, we brainstormed. But I did all the grunt work of writing the thing. And he made me do 16 versions of an ad that was almost there. So it wasn't, it was good enough. But it wasn't perfect. And he needed it to be perfect because he was going up against other A-list writers. In that game with A-list writers that the places like boardroom and Rodeil and Abora, because they have such good writers, you need to be a little more perfect in the average entrepreneur. Because you're going to win by 0.01%. And that will become the control. And you can lose by 0.01%. And a near perfect letter can become Trash Me, just because it got deep by a set. That's why those are the guys you really want to pay attention to. You want to be buying from Rodeil. You want to be buying from boardroom. Those are the best ways. You want to be buying from Agora. Because they got the top, they're getting the top, the top talent. Oh, I'd say at that time there were 15 direct response writers worthy of writing for those guys in the world. There's probably a few more now. But it always seems to settle down to just a handful to figure it out and willing to do that. But you don't need to be A-list. You need to understand what they're doing. But you don't need to approach that. But it's always good to know what the best and the best are doing. All right. Thank John. You all have what?
